title: What is depression?
citation: American Psychiatric Association (2022). Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (5th ed., text revision). APA Publishing.
content:
Depression is a serious and common medical condition that affects how a person feels, thinks, and handles daily activities. It is more than a passing low mood. While everyone experiences periods of sadness or low energy, depression involves a persistent and pervasive change in mood and functioning that lasts for weeks, months, or longer and that significantly interferes with everyday life.

The defining features of depression include persistently low or depressed mood, and a loss of interest or pleasure in activities that were once enjoyable, a symptom known as anhedonia. These two core features are accompanied by a range of other symptoms that vary in their combination and severity from person to person. Sleep disturbances are common, whether that means sleeping too much or finding it impossible to sleep. Appetite and weight may change substantially. Energy levels tend to drop, and even ordinary tasks can feel exhausting or overwhelming. Concentration and memory are often affected, making work, study, or decision-making feel much harder than usual. Some people experience a slowing of thought and movement that is noticeable to others.

Depression is not a character flaw or a sign of weakness. It is not something a person can simply decide to push through by trying harder or thinking more positively. Biological, psychological, and social factors all interact in the development of depression. Changes in how certain neurotransmitters function, life events including loss and trauma, chronic stress, physical illness, and genetic vulnerability all play roles. In many cases, depression develops without any single identifiable cause.

Depression is one of the leading causes of disability worldwide. It is also highly treatable. With appropriate support, including psychological therapy, medication, lifestyle interventions, or a combination of these approaches, the great majority of people with depression experience significant improvement.
